front: What is the fundamental principle behind the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm?
back: BFS explores a graph level-by-level. It starts at the root node and explores all neighboring nodes at the present depth before moving on to nodes at the next depth level. It uses a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue to manage the nodes to be explored.

front: What data structure is essential for implementing BFS and what is its purpose?
back: A FIFO (First-In, First-Out) Queue. It is used to keep track of all the nodes that have been discovered but not yet explored, ensuring that nodes are visited in the order they are found (the oldest unvisited nodes first).

front: What are the key properties of BFS regarding pathfinding and completeness?
back: BFS is complete (will find a solution if one exists) and optimal (will find the shortest path) for unweighted graphs. This is because it always finds the solution with the fewest edges from the start node.

front: What is the time and space complexity of BFS when applied to a graph?
back: Time Complexity: O(|V| + |E|), where |V| is the number of vertices and |E| is the number of edges. Space Complexity: O(|V|) to store the queue and visited nodes, which in the worst case can store all nodes.

front: In BFS, what is the purpose of maintaining a 'visited' list or set?
back: To mark nodes that have already been encountered and added to the queue. This prevents the algorithm from getting stuck in an infinite loop by re-visiting the same nodes and processing them multiple times.
